question: Question 1. Tell me about yourself
answer:
I'm a frontend developer who has spent the last several years working mostly in Next.js, building production apps that need both good user experience and strong SEO.

I've worked across the framework's evolution — from the older Pages Router with getStaticProps and getServerSideProps, through the shift to the App Router with React Server Components, Server Actions, and streaming. I've shipped marketing sites that need to rank well in search, dashboards that need fast client interactivity, and e-commerce storefronts that need a mix of both.

I care a lot about picking the right rendering strategy for each part of an app rather than defaulting to one approach everywhere — some pages want static generation, some want server rendering per request, and some are better as fully client-rendered widgets.

I've also spent time on the operational side — deploying to Vercel, but also self-hosting with the Node adapter, setting up caching correctly, and debugging performance issues related to hydration and bundle size.
bonus: I like Next.js specifically because it doesn't force one rendering model — it gives you the tools to mix static, server, and client rendering in the same app, and a lot of the skill is knowing when to reach for each one.

question: Question 2. What does Next.js give you on top of plain React?
answer:
Plain React is just a UI library — it renders components, but it doesn't know anything about routing, data fetching conventions, or how your app gets built and served. Next.js is a framework built around React that fills in those gaps.

It gives you file-based routing, so the folder structure of your project defines your URLs instead of you wiring up a router by hand. It gives you multiple rendering strategies out of the box — server-side rendering, static generation, and incremental static regeneration — instead of shipping everything as a client-rendered single-page app.

It also handles bundling and code splitting per route automatically, optimizes images and fonts through built-in components, and now, with the App Router, lets you write React Server Components that run only on the server and never ship their code to the browser.

Basically, React gives you the component model, and Next.js gives you the application layer around it — routing, rendering, and build tooling — so you're not assembling all of that yourself from separate libraries.
bonus: A lot of teams used to hand-roll this stack themselves with React Router, webpack config, and a custom Express server. Next.js's main pitch is that it gives you a well-integrated version of all of that with much less setup.

question: Question 3. How does file-based routing work in Next.js?
answer:
In Next.js, the files and folders inside your routes directory directly map to URLs — you don't write a router configuration by hand.

In the App Router, you create a folder for each route segment, and inside it a page file, like a file named page.js, which becomes the actual page component for that route. So a folder called about containing a page file becomes the slash about route.

In the older Pages Router, it was even more direct — a file called about.js inside the pages folder became the slash about route, with no extra folder needed.

Nested folders create nested URL segments, so a dashboard folder containing a settings folder containing a page file becomes slash dashboard slash settings. This convention removes an entire category of routing configuration that other frameworks need you to write manually.
bonus: Because routing is tied to the file system, it's very easy to look at a project's folder structure and immediately understand its URL structure, which is a nice side benefit for onboarding new developers.

question: Question 4. What is a layout in the App Router and how is it different from a page?
answer:
A page represents the unique content for a specific route — it's the thing that changes when you navigate from one URL to another.

A layout is UI that wraps a page and any nested routes underneath it, and it persists across navigations within that segment — it doesn't re-render or lose its state when you move between child pages. A common example is a dashboard layout that renders a sidebar and header once, while the main content area swaps between different pages as the user navigates.

Layouts can be nested — a root layout wraps the entire app, and you can add more specific layouts further down the folder tree that only apply to a subsection of routes. Because a layout stays mounted across navigation, things like scroll position in a sidebar or an open state in a nested menu are preserved automatically, which you'd otherwise have to manage manually.

The root layout is also mandatory in the App Router — it's where you put your html and body tags, since Next.js no longer uses a separate custom document file for that.
bonus: Because layouts don't re-render on navigation, they're a natural place to fetch data that's shared across many pages, like the current user's profile, without refetching it on every click.

question: Question 5. What's the difference between the Pages Router and the App Router, and why did Next.js introduce the App Router?
answer:
The Pages Router is the original Next.js routing system, based on a pages folder where each file is a route and every component in it is a client component by default, rendered on the server for the initial request and then hydrated in the browser.

The App Router is the newer system, based on an app folder, built around React Server Components. In the App Router, components are server components by default — they run only on the server and don't add to the JavaScript bundle — and you opt into client-side interactivity explicitly with a directive at the top of a file.

The App Router also introduces nested layouts, colocated loading and error states, streaming with Suspense, and Server Actions for mutations, none of which existed cleanly in the Pages Router.

Next.js introduced it because the Pages Router had real limitations — every page shipped its entire component tree as client-side JavaScript even if most of it was static, data fetching was awkward for nested layouts, and there was no built-in way to stream parts of a page independently. The App Router was designed to solve those problems using React's own Server Components model rather than Next-specific workarounds.
bonus: Both routers can coexist in the same project during a migration — Next.js checks the app folder first and falls back to pages, which is what makes gradual App Router adoption practical for large codebases.

question: Question 6. How do dynamic routes and catch-all routes work?
answer:
A dynamic route lets a single file handle many URLs by capturing part of the path as a parameter. You create it by naming a folder or file with square brackets, so a folder called bracket id bracket becomes a route that matches any value in that position, like slash products slash 42, and gives you access to that value as a parameter in your component.

A catch-all route captures multiple path segments at once instead of just one. You write it with three dots inside the brackets, so bracket dot dot dot slug bracket matches slash docs slash a, slash docs slash a slash b, and so on, giving you an array of all the matched segments.

There's also an optional catch-all route, written with double brackets around the same syntax, which additionally matches the base path with no extra segments at all — so it would also match plain slash docs, not just paths with something after it.

These are especially useful for things like a CMS-driven site where you don't know the full page structure ahead of time — a single catch-all route file can render any arbitrarily nested content path.
bonus: A common real use case is documentation sites — one bracket dot dot dot slug bracket route reads a markdown file path from the URL segments and renders whatever content matches, without needing a route defined for every single page.

question: Question 7. What is middleware in Next.js and what would you use it for?
answer:
Middleware is code that runs before a request reaches your route, defined in a single middleware file at the root of your project. It runs on the edge runtime by default, so it's fast and can execute close to the user before any page rendering happens.

You use it for cross-cutting concerns that apply across many routes — checking authentication and redirecting unauthenticated users to a login page, reading and setting cookies, doing A B test bucketing, rewriting requests based on geography or locale, or adding security headers to every response.

Middleware can inspect the incoming request, and then choose to let it continue, redirect it, rewrite it to a different path, or return a response directly, without ever reaching the actual page component.

You configure which paths it applies to with a matcher, so you're not running it on every single request in the app, like static assets, unless you actually need to.
bonus: Because middleware runs on the edge runtime, it doesn't have access to full Node APIs — so things like direct database drivers usually don't belong there. It's better suited to lightweight checks like reading a cookie than to heavy business logic.

question: Question 8. How does navigation work with next link and the navigation hooks, and why not just use a regular anchor tag?
answer:
The Link component from next slash link is how you navigate between pages in a Next.js app. It renders an actual anchor tag under the hood, so it still works for things like right-click open in new tab, but it also intercepts normal clicks to do client-side navigation instead of a full page reload.

When a Link is visible in the viewport, Next.js automatically prefetches the linked page in the background, so by the time the user actually clicks it, the page's data and code are often already loaded, making the transition feel instant.

For navigation triggered by code rather than a click — like redirecting after a form submits — you use the router from the use router hook, which gives you methods like push and replace. In the App Router, that hook comes from next slash navigation rather than next slash router, which was the Pages Router version.

A plain anchor tag would work, but it causes a full browser navigation — the whole page reloads, all JavaScript re-downloads and re-executes, and you lose any in-memory state. Using Link keeps the single-page-app feel while still producing real, crawlable links.
bonus: Next slash navigation also gives you hooks like use pathname and use search params for reading the current URL inside client components, which replaced several separate APIs from the Pages Router's use router hook.

question: Question 9. What are the differences between server-side rendering, static generation, and incremental static regeneration?
answer:
Static generation means the HTML for a page is built once, at build time, and then served as a static file to every visitor. It's the fastest option because there's no server work happening per request — you're just serving a file, often from a CDN.

Server-side rendering means the HTML is generated fresh on the server for every single request. It's useful when the content genuinely depends on that specific request — things like a logged-in user's personalized dashboard, or content that must always be up to the second.

Incremental static regeneration is a middle ground — the page is generated statically like normal static generation, but Next.js will regenerate it in the background after a set amount of time or on demand, without you needing to rebuild and redeploy the whole site. Visitors keep getting the fast, cached version while a fresh version is being generated behind the scenes.

The general decision process is: if content is the same for everyone and doesn't change often, use static generation or incremental static regeneration. If it changes on every request or depends on request-specific data like cookies, use server-side rendering.
bonus: A blog is the classic incremental static regeneration example — you want the speed of a static page, but you don't want to redeploy the entire site every time an editor fixes a typo in one article.

question: Question 10. How would you decide which rendering strategy to use for a given page in an app?
answer:
I start by asking whether the content is the same for every visitor. If it is — a marketing page, a product listing, a blog post — static generation is usually the right default, because it's the cheapest and fastest option to serve.

If the content changes periodically but doesn't need to be instant, like product prices that update every few minutes or blog content edited through a CMS, incremental static regeneration lets me keep the speed of static pages while still staying reasonably fresh, without rebuilding the whole site on every content change.

If the content is truly personalized or request-specific — a user's account page, search results based on query parameters, anything that reads cookies or headers to decide what to render — I reach for server-side rendering, or in the App Router, a server component that fetches fresh data on every request.

And if something is highly interactive and doesn't need to be indexed by search engines at all — a complex settings form, a live chat widget — it's often fine to just render it fully on the client, since the SEO and time-to-first-byte benefits of the other strategies don't matter there.
bonus: It's rarely an all-or-nothing choice for an entire app — most real Next.js apps mix all four approaches across different routes, matching each page to what it actually needs.

question: Question 11. What were getStaticProps and getServerSideProps, and what replaced them in the App Router?
answer:
In the Pages Router, getStaticProps and getServerSideProps were special exported functions you'd put in a page file to control data fetching and rendering strategy.

getStaticProps ran at build time, fetched whatever data the page needed, and Next.js used the returned data to generate static HTML. Adding a revalidate value to its return object turned it into incremental static regeneration, telling Next.js to regenerate that page after the specified number of seconds.

getServerSideProps ran on every single request, on the server, and its return value became the page's props — this is what gave you server-side rendering with fresh, request-specific data every time.

In the App Router, there's no separate data-fetching function like this at all — you just fetch data directly inside a server component using async and await, right in the component body. The rendering strategy is now controlled by how you call fetch — its caching options — and by route-level configuration, rather than by which special function you export.
bonus: This change is a big simplification conceptually — instead of learning two separate function names with different semantics, you write one mental model: async server components with configurable fetch caching.

question: Question 12. How do you trigger revalidation for a statically generated page, and what are the two main approaches?
answer:
There are two main revalidation approaches: time-based and on-demand.

Time-based revalidation is the simpler one — you set a revalidate value, either on a fetch call or at the route segment level, and Next.js will treat the cached page or data as stale after that many seconds. The next request after that point triggers a regeneration in the background, while still serving the stale version instantly to that request — this pattern is called stale while revalidate.

On-demand revalidation is triggered by your own code, usually from a Server Action or a Route Handler, calling a function like revalidate path or revalidate tag with the specific path or tag you want to invalidate. This is useful when you know exactly when content changed — for example, right after an editor publishes an article in a CMS, you can immediately revalidate that specific article's page instead of waiting for a timer.

In practice, a lot of production apps use on-demand revalidation triggered by a webhook from their content management system, which gives near-instant updates without sacrificing the performance benefits of static generation the rest of the time.
bonus: On-demand revalidation is usually the better choice when you have a clear signal for when data changed, since time-based revalidation always involves either serving slightly stale content or revalidating more often than necessary.

question: Question 13. What is streaming server-side rendering and how does it help with performance?
answer:
Traditional server-side rendering waits until the entire page's data is fetched and the entire HTML is generated before sending anything to the browser. If one slow piece of data holds things up, the whole page waits on it.

Streaming server-side rendering sends HTML to the browser in pieces, as it becomes ready, instead of all at once. The parts of the page that are fast to render — like a header or a static layout — get sent immediately, while slower parts stream in afterward as their data resolves.

In the App Router, this is built on React Suspense — you wrap a slow component in a Suspense boundary with a fallback, and Next.js sends the fallback first, then streams in the real content and swaps it in once it's ready, without the user needing to wait on a blank screen or a full-page spinner.

This matters a lot for perceived performance — the user sees something useful much sooner, even if the full page technically isn't finished loading yet.
bonus: Streaming also helps with a single slow dependency not blocking the whole response — if one API call is slow, only the part of the page depending on it is delayed, not the entire page.

question: Question 14. What are the trade-offs between client-side rendering and server-side rendering in a Next.js app?
answer:
Server-side rendering sends fully formed HTML to the browser, so the user sees content immediately without waiting for JavaScript to download and execute. It's also better for search engine crawlers, since they see real content in the initial response rather than an empty shell that needs JavaScript to fill in.

Client-side rendering sends a mostly empty HTML shell along with JavaScript, and the actual content gets rendered in the browser after that JavaScript runs. This adds a delay before the user sees meaningful content, but it can make sense for highly interactive parts of an app where content is genuinely dynamic and personalized on every interaction, like a real-time dashboard that updates constantly regardless of any single request.

Server rendering also has a cost — it uses server resources for every request, and if your data fetching is slow, that latency shows up directly in time to first byte, unlike client rendering where at least something shows up quickly even if it's just a loading state.

In practice, Next.js apps aren't purely one or the other — server components render the initial structure and content, and client components handle the genuinely interactive pieces, giving you the SEO and speed benefits of server rendering with the interactivity of client rendering where it's actually needed.
bonus: A common mistake is marking way more components as client components than necessary, out of habit from older React apps — that quietly pushes work back onto the client and undoes a lot of what server rendering is trying to buy you.

question: Question 15. What is hydration, and why can it be a performance bottleneck?
answer:
Hydration is the process where React takes the static HTML that was already rendered on the server and attaches its event listeners and internal state to it in the browser, turning static markup into a fully interactive React application.

Before hydration finishes, the page looks complete and functional to the eye, but things like button clicks or form inputs won't actually do anything yet, because React hasn't wired up its handlers. This is why you can sometimes click a button on a freshly loaded page and nothing happens — the visual page loaded, but hydration hasn't caught up.

Hydration can become a performance bottleneck because it requires downloading, parsing, and executing JavaScript for every component that needs to become interactive, even ones that were already visually complete from the server-rendered HTML. On a page with a lot of components, this can tie up the main thread and delay interactivity, which shows up in metrics like time to interactive.

React Server Components help here specifically because components that never need interactivity don't ship any JavaScript at all, so there's nothing to hydrate for them — only the client components that actually need event handlers add to that hydration cost.
bonus: This is part of why the App Router pushes you toward server components by default — every component you keep as a server component is one less thing contributing to the hydration bill on the client.

question: Question 16. What is the App Router equivalent of the old getStaticPaths function used for dynamic static routes?
answer:
In the Pages Router, if you had a dynamic route like a bracket slug bracket page and wanted it statically generated for a known set of values, you exported a function called get static paths that returned the list of parameter values Next.js should pre-render at build time.

In the App Router, the equivalent is a function called generate static params, exported from the page file. It returns an array of objects, where each object's keys match the dynamic segment names in the route, and Next.js uses that list to pre-render each of those specific pages at build time.

For example, for a route folder named bracket slug bracket, generate static params might fetch a list of all blog post slugs and return them so each individual post page gets statically generated ahead of time instead of on first request.

Any param values not included in that returned list are still handled — by default Next.js will render them on demand and can cache the result afterward, similar in spirit to what fallback true used to do in the Pages Router version.
bonus: You can also opt into strictly limiting generation to only the params you list, using a dynamic params setting, which is useful if you want a hard guarantee that unknown values return a not found page instead of being rendered on demand.

question: Question 17. What is the fundamental difference between Server Components and Client Components?
answer:
A Server Component runs only on the server. It can do things like directly query a database, read a file, or use secret API keys, and none of that code — not even the fact that it exists — is ever sent to the browser. Only the resulting rendered output is sent down as part of the response.

A Client Component runs in the browser, just like components in a traditional React app. It's what you use when you need interactivity — click handlers, state with use state, effects with use effect, or browser-only APIs like local storage.

In the App Router, every component is a Server Component by default, unless you explicitly mark it otherwise. This is a big shift from the Pages Router, where every component was effectively a client component that also happened to render on the server for the first request.

The practical benefit is that Server Components add zero JavaScript to the client bundle — they're not shipped, they're not hydrated, they just produce HTML. That means you can use large libraries for things like markdown parsing or date formatting inside a Server Component without paying any client bundle size cost for them at all.
bonus: A useful mental habit is to write everything as a Server Component first, and only convert a piece to a Client Component when you hit something that genuinely requires the browser — interactivity, state, or a browser-only API.

question: Question 18. What does the 'use client' directive actually do, and where do you put it?
answer:
The use client directive is a string placed at the very top of a file, before any imports, and it marks that file and everything it imports as part of the client bundle — meaning it will be sent to the browser and hydrated there.

It doesn't mean the component only renders on the client — a client component still gets server-rendered to HTML for the initial page load, just like before. What it changes is that its JavaScript is also shipped to the browser so it can hydrate and become interactive, and it gains access to client-only features like state, effects, and event handlers.

A common point of confusion is thinking use client creates a boundary around just that one component, but it actually applies to the whole module graph from that file downward — every component that file imports also becomes part of that client bundle, unless that imported component is passed in as a child from a server component instead of imported directly.

Because of that, the recommendation is to push use client as far down the component tree as possible — wrap only the small interactive piece, like a button or a form, rather than marking a large page-level component as a client component just because one small part of it needs interactivity.
bonus: You can still pass Server Components as children into a Client Component through the children prop — the client component doesn't need to import them directly, so this pattern lets you keep most of a tree server-rendered even while wrapping it with an interactive client shell.

question: Question 19. What are Server Actions and what problem do they solve?
answer:
Server Actions are functions that run on the server but can be called directly from a Client Component, as if they were a normal function call, without you having to manually build a Route Handler and fetch it yourself.

You mark a function as a Server Action with a use server directive, either at the top of the function or at the top of a whole file. When a client component calls it — say, as the action attached to a form, or from a button's click handler — Next.js handles serializing the arguments, making the actual network request, and returning the result, all behind a normal-looking function call in your code.

They solve the problem of mutations feeling disconnected from the rest of your app. Before Server Actions, to save a form you'd write a separate API route, then a fetch call to it on the client, then wire up loading and error states by hand. With Server Actions, the form submission handler is just a server function, and it can directly call revalidate path afterward to refresh the data shown on the page, all colocated with the rest of your component logic.

They also work with progressive enhancement — a form using a Server Action as its action prop can actually submit and work even before JavaScript has finished loading, because the browser's native form submission handles it as a fallback.
bonus: Server Actions still go through validation on the server — since anything callable from the client is effectively a public endpoint, you still need to validate input and check authorization inside the action itself, just like you would for any API route.

question: Question 20. What are loading.js, error.js, and not-found.js used for in the App Router?
answer:
These are special files you can add alongside a page file in a route folder, and Next.js automatically wires them up for that specific route segment without any manual configuration.

A loading file defines a loading UI that's shown automatically while the page or its data is still being fetched. Behind the scenes, Next.js wraps the page in a Suspense boundary using that file as the fallback, so you get a streaming loading state for free just by adding the file.

An error file defines a fallback UI shown if something throws during rendering in that route segment. It works like an automatically wired-up error boundary, and it receives the error and a reset function you can use to let the user retry, without crashing the rest of the app outside that segment.

A not-found file defines what renders when a route can't be resolved, or when your own code explicitly calls the not found function to signal that the requested resource doesn't exist — for example, a product page for an ID that isn't in the database.

What's nice about this convention is that these behaviors are scoped to the specific route segment they live in, so a deeply nested route can have its own error and loading UI without affecting the rest of the app, and without you writing any boundary-wiring code yourself.
bonus: Because error boundaries only catch errors during rendering, an error file won't catch something like a failed fetch inside an event handler — for that you still need normal try and catch logic in the client code.

question: Question 21. What are parallel routes and intercepting routes, and what problem do they solve?
answer:
Parallel routes let you render more than one page simultaneously within the same layout, each independently, using named slots. You create a slot by naming a folder with an at sign, like an at sign modal folder, and the layout can render each of these named slots as its own prop, so multiple independent pieces of UI can load and update on their own without one blocking the other.

Intercepting routes let you show a route as an overlay on top of the current page when navigation happens from within the app, while still rendering as a genuinely separate full page if the user loads that URL directly — say, from a shared link or a page refresh.

A classic example that motivates both features is a photo gallery like the ones on Instagram or Twitter. When you click a photo from the feed, it opens as a modal overlaid on the feed behind it, and the URL updates to a distinct photo URL. But if someone visits that same URL directly, they get a proper full page for that photo instead of a modal with nothing behind it. Intercepting routes handle the modal-over-existing-page behavior, and parallel routes give you the slot structure to render that modal alongside the main feed content.

Before these features, this kind of pattern needed a lot of manual client-side state management to fake, and it didn't produce a real, shareable URL for the modal content the way this approach does.
bonus: These two features are usually used together rather than separately — intercepting routes provide the modal-over-page behavior, and parallel route slots provide the layout structure needed to actually render that modal next to the page it's covering.

question: Question 22. What are route groups and why would you use them?
answer:
A route group is a folder whose name is wrapped in parentheses, like a folder called open paren marketing close paren. The parentheses tell Next.js to use that folder for organization only — it does not become part of the actual URL path.

This lets you organize related routes together, or give a group of routes their own shared layout, without that grouping leaking into the URLs users actually see. For example, you might have one route group for marketing pages with a simple layout containing a header and footer, and a separate route group for the authenticated app with a completely different layout containing a sidebar and navigation — both living side by side in the folder structure, but with URLs that look flat and normal, with no marketing or app segment showing up in the path.

Route groups are also useful for scoping a root layout differently for different sections of an app, since each route group can define its own layout file that applies only to the routes nested inside it.

A route group can be a helpful tool for large apps where you want clean separation between, say, a public site and an authenticated dashboard, while keeping the URLs simple and not artificially nested.
bonus: You can even have multiple route groups define separate root layouts entirely, letting one section of a site look completely different from another — like a totally different html structure for a docs subsection versus the main marketing site — while still being part of the same Next.js project.

question: Question 23. What's the difference between layout.js and template.js?
answer:
A layout persists across navigations between the routes nested inside it — React preserves its component state and doesn't re-mount it when the user moves between child pages. This is what makes layouts good for things like a sidebar that shouldn't lose its scroll position or expanded state as the user clicks around.

A template looks similar and is used the same way structurally, but it does not persist — React creates a brand new instance of it on every navigation, meaning its state resets and any effects re-run each time.

This makes templates useful for specific cases where you actually want fresh behavior on every navigation — for example, an effect that logs a page view every time the route changes, or a page transition animation that should replay every time, neither of which would work correctly if the component instance stayed the same the way a layout's does.

Most of the time, layout is what you want, since preserving state and avoiding unnecessary remounts is usually the better default — template is more of a special-purpose tool for the specific cases where remounting on every navigation is actually the desired behavior.
bonus: A layout and a template can coexist in the same route segment — Next.js renders the template inside the layout, so you get the persistent shell from the layout with the remount-on-navigation behavior from the template for whatever's inside it.

question: Question 24. How does React Suspense integrate with the App Router for streaming?
answer:
In the App Router, Suspense is the mechanism that powers streaming. When a Server Component performs an async operation, like fetching data with await, and that component is wrapped in a Suspense boundary, Next.js can send the rest of the page immediately and stream in that specific piece once its data resolves.

You wrap a slow component in a Suspense tag with a fallback, and Next.js sends the fallback content as part of the initial HTML, then swaps in the real content over that same slot once the async work finishes, without needing a page reload or a client-side loading spinner state managed manually.

This lets you have multiple independent Suspense boundaries on the same page, each resolving on its own timeline — a fast piece of content shows up right away, while a slower piece continues showing its fallback and streams in separately, without either one blocking the other.

The automatic loading file convention is actually built entirely on top of this same mechanism — Next.js wraps your whole page in a Suspense boundary using that file as the fallback, so even if you never write a Suspense tag yourself, you're already using this system any time you add a loading file to a route.
bonus: This is a meaningful shift from older React patterns where Suspense was mostly discussed for client-side code splitting with lazy — in the App Router, Suspense's main real-world use is for streaming server-rendered data-dependent content.

question: Question 25. How would you structure Suspense boundaries on a page with multiple independent data sources?
answer:
I'd avoid wrapping the entire page in one giant Suspense boundary, because that means the whole page waits on the slowest piece of data before showing anything at all — even parts that were ready instantly.

Instead, I break the page into separate components based on their data dependencies, and wrap each slow one in its own Suspense boundary with its own fallback. That way a fast header can render immediately, while a slower recommendations section streams in later on its own, and neither blocks the other.

For a typical product page, that might mean the core product details render right away since that data is fast and critical, while a reviews section and a related products section each get their own Suspense boundary further down the page, since those calls are slower and less critical for the user's first impression.

The general principle is to think about what content is important enough that the user should wait for it before seeing anything, versus what content is fine to arrive a moment later — and structure your boundaries around that priority rather than around arbitrary component boundaries.
bonus: It also helps to keep the fallback UI visually similar in shape to the real content, like a skeleton that matches the layout, so the streaming swap doesn't cause a jarring layout shift when the real content appears.

question: Question 26. How does the metadata API work for SEO in the App Router?
answer:
The metadata API replaces manually writing head tags yourself, and instead lets you export a metadata object or a generate metadata function from a page or layout file, which Next.js uses to build the actual head tags for that route.

A static metadata object works for pages where the title and description don't depend on data — you just export an object with fields like title and description, and Next.js renders the corresponding meta tags automatically.

For pages where metadata depends on dynamic data — like a product page where the title should include the actual product name — you export an async function called generate metadata instead, which fetches whatever data it needs and returns the metadata object built from that data. It runs on the server, separately from the page component itself, though Next.js is smart enough to deduplicate a fetch call that's requested identically in both places.

Metadata also supports nested merging — a layout can define shared metadata like a site name, and a page further down can override or extend specific fields, similar to how layouts and pages nest for UI.
bonus: The metadata API also has dedicated support for things like Open Graph images and Twitter cards, including a way to generate a dynamic Open Graph image per page using a special image generation file, which used to require a lot of manual setup with a separate image rendering service.

question: Question 27. How does fetch caching work by default in Next.js, and how do you control it?
answer:
Next.js extends the native fetch function with its own caching layer. In the Pages Router era and early App Router versions, fetch calls inside Server Components were cached by default, similar in spirit to static generation, unless you told it otherwise. In more recent versions, the default flipped — fetch requests are no longer cached automatically unless you opt in, which better matches what most people expect from a plain fetch call.

You control this per fetch call with an options object. Setting cache to force cache tells Next.js to cache the response and reuse it for subsequent requests, similar to static generation. Setting cache to no store tells it to always fetch fresh data on every request, similar to server-side rendering with no caching at all.

You can also add a next options field with a revalidate value in seconds, which caches the response but automatically refetches and updates it after that time passes — the same time-based revalidation idea used for full pages, but scoped to just that specific fetch call.

Because behavior has changed across versions, this is one of the areas where it really matters to check which exact Next.js version a project is on, since assuming the wrong default can lead to either serving unexpectedly stale data or unexpectedly hitting your backend on every single request.
bonus: You can also tag a fetch call with a next options field called tags, and then later call revalidate tag with that same tag name to invalidate every cached fetch that used it, without needing to know every specific path that depended on that data.

question: Question 28. What are Route Handlers and how are they different from the old API routes?
answer:
Route Handlers are the App Router's way of defining backend endpoints. You create a file named route dot js inside a route folder, and export functions named after HTTP methods, like get, post, or delete, each of which handles requests of that type for that path.

The old API routes, from the Pages Router, lived in a special pages slash api folder, and each file exported a single default handler function that received a request and response object and had to branch internally based on the method, similar to how a plain Express handler would look.

Route Handlers use the standard web request and response objects rather than the older Node-style request and response objects that API routes used, which makes them feel more aligned with general web platform APIs rather than something Next-specific.

Route Handlers also support both the Node runtime and the edge runtime, letting you choose per route depending on whether you need full Node APIs or want the lower latency and different constraints of running at the edge, whereas old API routes only ran in the traditional Node environment.
bonus: Route Handlers can't be nested inside a folder that already has a page file for the same route — a segment is either a page or a Route Handler, not both, which keeps the routing model unambiguous.

question: Question 29. What's the difference between revalidatePath and revalidateTag?
answer:
Both functions let you manually invalidate cached data from inside a Server Action or a Route Handler, rather than waiting for a time-based revalidation window to pass, but they target the cache differently.

revalidate path invalidates the cache for a specific route, identified by its path, like slash blog slash my post. After calling it, the next request to that path regenerates fresh content instead of serving the previously cached version. It's straightforward when you know exactly which page needs to be refreshed.

revalidate tag invalidates every cached fetch call anywhere in the app that was tagged with that specific tag name, regardless of which page or route it happened to be part of. This is more powerful when the same underlying data shows up on multiple different pages — for example, a product's price might appear on its own product page, on a category listing page, and on a homepage featured section, all as separate fetch calls. Tagging all of those fetches with the product's ID and calling revalidate tag with that ID invalidates all of them at once, without you needing to individually track and call revalidate path for every page that happened to display that product.

In general, revalidate path is simpler for single-page invalidation, and revalidate tag is better when data is reused across multiple pages and you want a single invalidation call to reach all of them.
bonus: A practical pattern is tagging fetches by resource type and ID, like a tag combining the word product with the specific ID, so a webhook from a CMS or database update can revalidate exactly the right tag without needing to know every page that data appears on.

question: Question 30. How do you access a database directly from a Server Component, and is that actually a good idea?
answer:
Because Server Components run only on the server, you can import a database client or an ORM directly inside one and query the database right there in the component, using async and await, without going through an API route at all. There's no risk of exposing the database credentials or the query logic to the browser, since none of that code is ever sent to the client.

This is a real shift from the traditional pattern of building a separate API layer just so the frontend has something to fetch from — for a lot of pages, especially ones that are purely for reading and displaying data, that intermediate API layer becomes unnecessary overhead.

Whether it's a good idea depends on the shape of the app. For a page that only needs to render data for that one request, querying directly in the Server Component is simpler and has fewer moving parts. But if the same data access logic needs to be shared with other consumers — a mobile app, a public API, other services — then centralizing that logic behind an actual API layer, or at least a shared data access module, still makes sense so you're not duplicating query logic across every place that needs it.

Most real projects land somewhere in between — they pull data access into shared functions that Server Components call directly, keeping the convenience of no extra network hop while still avoiding scattering raw queries across every single component.
bonus: This pattern doesn't remove the need for validation and authorization — even though there's no API route boundary anymore, you still need to check that the current user is allowed to see or modify whatever data the component is about to query.

question: Question 31. How should you handle environment variables and secrets in a Next.js app?
answer:
Next.js reads environment variables from files like dot env local, and by default all of them are only available on the server — inside Server Components, Server Actions, and Route Handlers — and never sent to the browser.

If you need a value available in client-side code, you have to explicitly prefix its name with next public. Only variables with that specific prefix get inlined into the client JavaScript bundle at build time — everything else stays server-only, which is an intentional safety mechanism so you don't accidentally leak a secret just by referencing it somewhere.

Because of that prefix rule, actual secrets — database credentials, private API keys, signing secrets — should never use the next public prefix, and ideally should only ever be read inside server-only code, never passed down into a client component's props even accidentally, since anything that ends up in props for a client component does get serialized and sent to the browser.

For different environments, you typically use different env files, like dot env production or dot env development, or more commonly, set the actual secret values through your hosting platform's environment variable configuration rather than committing any real secrets into the repository at all.
bonus: A good habit is to keep a dot env example file checked into the repo listing every variable name the app needs, with placeholder or empty values, so new developers know exactly what to configure without ever seeing a real secret.

question: Question 32. What's the difference between the edge runtime and the Node runtime for Route Handlers, and when would you choose each?
answer:
The Node runtime is the traditional, full Node dot js environment — it supports the complete Node API surface, including things like native database drivers, file system access, and most npm packages without restriction.

The edge runtime is a much lighter, more restricted JavaScript environment, based on web standard APIs rather than Node APIs. It's designed to run in many geographically distributed locations close to users, which gives it lower latency, but it doesn't support things like direct TCP database connections, the file system, or many Node-specific APIs and packages.

I'd choose the edge runtime for lightweight, latency-sensitive logic — things like reading a cookie, doing a redirect, calling an external HTTP API, or simple authentication checks — where the code doesn't need Node-specific capabilities and benefits from running physically closer to the user.

I'd stick with the Node runtime for anything that needs a real database connection through a traditional driver, heavier computation, or any npm package that assumes a full Node environment, since trying to force that kind of logic onto the edge runtime usually means fighting its restrictions instead of benefiting from them.
bonus: Many modern database providers now offer edge-compatible drivers built over HTTP instead of raw TCP connections specifically so you can query them from edge functions — but it's still worth checking a driver's documentation before assuming it works there.

question: Question 33. How do you read query parameters, headers, and the request body inside a Route Handler?
answer:
A Route Handler receives the incoming request as a standard web request object, so you read the request body the same way you would with any fetch-based API — calling a method like request dot json to parse a JSON body, or request dot form data for form submissions.

For query parameters, since the request object exposes its full URL, you can construct a URL object from request dot url and then read values off its search params, which gives you a standard way to pull out individual query string values by name.

For headers, the request object has a headers property that behaves like the standard web headers interface, so you call a get method with the header name to read something like an authorization header or a custom header sent by a client.

For the response, you construct and return a standard response object, or use the Next-specific response helper it re-exports, which gives convenient shortcuts like a json method for quickly returning a JSON response with the right content type header already set.
bonus: Because these are standard web APIs rather than Next-specific ones, a lot of this knowledge carries over directly if you've worked with fetch-based APIs, service workers, or other frameworks built on the same web standards, which wasn't true of the older Node-style API route objects.

question: Question 34. What's a good pattern for integrating a third-party API in a Next.js app, especially one that needs a secret key?
answer:
If the third-party API requires a secret key that should never reach the browser, I call it from server-only code — inside a Server Component for reads, or inside a Server Action or Route Handler for writes — so the key stays on the server the entire time.

For reads, that often means fetching the data directly inside a Server Component and passing the result down as props to whatever client components need to display it, rather than calling the third-party API from client-side code at all.

For writes triggered by user interaction — submitting a form that needs to call an external payment or email provider, for example — a Server Action is a natural fit, since it's already designed to be called from a client component while actually executing on the server, where the secret key can be safely used.

If multiple parts of the app need the same third-party integration, I'll usually wrap the actual API calls in a small server-only module — a set of functions that encapsulate the request logic, headers, and error handling for that specific service — so the integration details live in one place instead of being duplicated across every Server Action or Route Handler that needs to talk to that API.
bonus: It's worth double-checking that any client library provided by a third-party service is actually meant to run server-side — some SDKs are built assuming a browser environment and will behave unexpectedly, or fail outright, if you import them into server-only code without checking.

question: Question 35. How does the next Image component optimize images, and what problems does it solve compared to a plain img tag?
answer:
A plain img tag just loads whatever image file you point it at, at whatever size that file happens to be, and the browser has no built-in way to automatically serve a smaller version to a smaller screen or convert it to a more efficient format.

The Image component from next slash image automatically resizes images to the size actually needed for the layout, serves modern efficient formats like webp when the browser supports them, and lazy-loads images that are below the fold by default, so images the user hasn't scrolled to yet don't compete for bandwidth with what's currently visible.

It also requires you to specify width and height, or use the fill option with a sized parent container, which lets the browser reserve the correct amount of space for the image before it even loads — this prevents the page content from jumping around as images pop in, which is a real contributor to poor layout stability scores.

Under the hood, it generates a set of responsive image sizes and lets the browser pick the most appropriate one for the current viewport and device pixel ratio, instead of always downloading one fixed-size file regardless of whether the device actually needs that much resolution.
bonus: For an image that's critical to the initial view, like a large hero banner, you should set a priority prop on it, which tells Next.js to preload it and skip the default lazy-loading behavior, since lazy loading something the user sees immediately actually hurts perceived performance.

question: Question 36. How does font optimization work in Next.js?
answer:
Historically, loading web fonts from an external source like Google Fonts meant the browser had to make a separate network request to that third-party domain, which added latency and could cause a flash of unstyled text while the font was still loading.

Next.js has a built-in font system, next slash font, that downloads the font files at build time and self-hosts them alongside the rest of your static assets, so there's no runtime request to an external font provider at all, and no separate connection setup cost for that third-party domain.

It also automatically generates the right font-face CSS with size-adjust properties, which helps minimize layout shift between the fallback font shown initially and the real font once it loads, since the fallback is sized to closely match the final font's metrics.

You import a specific font function for the font family you want, configure options like weight and subset, and it gives you back a class name or style object to apply — the actual font files get bundled and served as static assets automatically, without you manually downloading font files or writing font-face CSS by hand.
bonus: Because the fonts are self-hosted as part of your own build output, there's also a privacy benefit — no request ever goes out to the third-party font provider's servers, which avoids that provider being able to see any information about your site's visitors.

question: Question 37. How would you analyze and reduce the JavaScript bundle size of a Next.js app?
answer:
I'd start by generating a visual breakdown of what's actually in the bundle, using a bundle analyzer tool that Next.js supports through a plugin — it produces a treemap showing exactly which packages and modules are taking up the most space, which is much more useful than guessing.

From there, common culprits are large libraries that are only used for a small piece of functionality, or a library that got imported in a way that pulls in its entire codebase instead of just the specific piece being used — swapping those for a lighter alternative, or importing just the specific function needed instead of the whole library, often makes a big difference.

I'd also check for client components that don't actually need to be client components — every component correctly kept as a Server Component contributes zero bytes to the client bundle, so converting something back to a Server Component, or moving the client-only piece further down the tree, directly reduces bundle size.

For genuinely large, non-critical pieces of a page, like a rich text editor or a charting library only needed after a user clicks something, dynamic imports let you split that code into its own chunk that's only downloaded when it's actually needed, instead of it inflating the size of the initial page load.
bonus: It's easy to accidentally undo a lot of this progress over time as new dependencies get added, so I like to check the bundle analyzer output periodically rather than only once at the start of a project.

question: Question 38. How does automatic code splitting work per route in Next.js?
answer:
Next.js automatically splits your app's JavaScript so that each route only loads the code it actually needs, rather than shipping one giant bundle containing every page in the entire app.

When a user visits a specific route, the browser downloads the JavaScript for that route, plus any shared code used across multiple routes, like your root layout or common utility functions — but not the code that belongs only to other, unrelated pages.

This happens automatically just from how the app is structured into separate route files — you don't have to manually configure which chunks exist, unlike in some older bundler setups where code splitting had to be configured by hand.

On top of that automatic per-route splitting, you can further split code within a single route using dynamic imports for specific components — useful for something like a heavy modal or chart component that isn't needed until the user interacts with the page, so it doesn't need to be part of that route's initial bundle at all.
bonus: This automatic splitting is part of why route-based navigation with next slash link feels fast even on large apps — a route's initial visit only pays for that route's own code, and prefetching means the next likely destination's chunk is often already loading in the background before the user even clicks.

question: Question 39. How does link prefetching work, and can it cause problems?
answer:
When a Link component is visible in the viewport, Next.js automatically starts prefetching the page it points to in the background — downloading its code, and depending on the page's caching setup, sometimes its data too — so that by the time the user actually clicks it, the navigation feels instant instead of waiting on a fresh request.

This works especially well for statically generated or cached pages, since prefetching a fully cached page is cheap and doesn't put extra load on your backend.

It can cause real problems if you have a page that's very expensive to render on every request — like one doing a genuinely heavy server-side computation with no caching — because if that page has many links pointing to it across the site, all of those visible links could trigger prefetch requests that each independently trigger that expensive work, adding real load even though no user actually navigated there yet.

In cases like that, I'd either add appropriate caching to that expensive page so prefetching it is cheap, or explicitly disable prefetching for links pointing to it by setting the prefetch prop to false, trading away the instant-navigation benefit for that specific link in exchange for not repeatedly triggering unnecessary backend work.
bonus: In the App Router, prefetching a dynamically rendered route by default just prefetches the shared layout down to the nearest loading boundary, not the full dynamic content — so the actual performance impact of prefetching a given page depends a lot on how that specific route is structured.

question: Question 40. What are Core Web Vitals and how does Next.js help with them specifically?
answer:
Core Web Vitals are a set of metrics Google uses to measure real-world user experience on a page — the main ones are largest contentful paint, which measures how long it takes for the biggest visible piece of content to render, cumulative layout shift, which measures how much visible content unexpectedly moves around as the page loads, and interaction to next paint, which measures how responsive the page feels when a user actually interacts with it.

Next.js helps with largest contentful paint through things like automatic image optimization and the ability to statically generate or cache pages, so the main content renders fast instead of waiting on slow server work or a bloated unoptimized image.

It helps with cumulative layout shift specifically because the Image component requires width and height up front, reserving the right amount of space before the image loads, and because font optimization reduces the visual jump between a fallback font and the final loaded font.

It helps with interaction responsiveness partly through Server Components reducing the amount of JavaScript that needs to load and hydrate on the client, since less JavaScript competing for the main thread generally means the page responds to interaction faster.

Next.js also ships a built-in way to actually measure these metrics in your own app, through a reporting function you can hook up to send this data to your own analytics, rather than only being able to check it manually in a lab tool.
bonus: It's worth remembering these are field metrics meant to reflect real users on real devices and networks — a fast result in a local dev environment doesn't guarantee good Core Web Vitals for someone on a slow connection or an older phone, so real user monitoring data matters more than lab testing alone.

question: Question 41. Explain the different caching layers in Next.js — the data cache, the full route cache, and the router cache.
answer:
The data cache sits at the level of individual fetch calls made on the server. When a fetch call opts into caching, its result is stored in this cache and reused across requests, instead of hitting the actual data source again — this is the layer controlled by the cache and revalidate options on fetch, and by revalidate tag and revalidate path.

The full route cache stores the actual rendered HTML and the underlying data payload for a statically rendered route, at build time or after regeneration. It's the layer that makes a fully static page fast to serve, since the whole rendering work has already been done ahead of time and this cache just stores the finished result.

The router cache is different — it lives in the browser, not on the server. As a user navigates around the app with client-side navigation, Next.js keeps a client-side cache of the route segments they've already visited, so navigating back to a page you were just on can be instant, reusing what's already in memory instead of refetching from the server.

These layers stack on top of each other and get invalidated somewhat independently, which is exactly why caching behavior in the App Router can feel confusing at first — a revalidate path call invalidates the data cache and the full route cache for that path, but the router cache in a user's browser might still show a slightly stale version until they trigger a hard refresh or enough time passes, depending on the specific version and configuration.
bonus: This layered design is powerful once it clicks, but it's also the single biggest source of confusing caching bugs reported by Next.js developers — when in doubt, checking exactly which of these three layers is actually serving the stale content is the fastest way to debug it.

question: Question 42. How would you reduce hydration cost on a page that has a lot of interactive widgets?
answer:
First, I'd check whether every one of those widgets actually needs to be a client component, or whether some of them are marked that way out of habit rather than genuine need — anything that doesn't need state, effects, or event handlers can move back to being a Server Component and stop contributing to hydration entirely.

For widgets that genuinely need interactivity but aren't needed immediately — something below the fold, or behind a tab that isn't selected by default — I'd use a dynamic import to load and hydrate that piece only when it's actually needed, instead of paying its hydration cost as part of the initial page load.

For a widget that's interactive but whose interactive part is actually small — like a static article with just one small share button that needs a click handler — I'd push the client boundary down to just that button rather than making the whole article a client component, so only that tiny piece needs to hydrate.

I'd also look at whether some client-side state can be replaced with something simpler, like relying on native browser behavior for something like a details and summary element instead of a fully custom React-controlled accordion, which removes the need for that particular widget to be a client component at all.
bonus: A good habit here is treating use client as an intentional, deliberate choice for the smallest possible piece of the tree — the fewer components carrying that directive, the less JavaScript there is to hydrate, and the faster the page becomes genuinely interactive.

question: Question 43. What's the difference between deploying a Next.js app to Vercel versus self-hosting it?
answer:
Vercel is the company that created Next.js, and their platform is built to match the framework very closely — features like incremental static regeneration, on-demand revalidation, edge functions, and image optimization all work out of the box with essentially zero configuration, because the platform is designed around exactly how Next.js expects to run.

Self-hosting means running the app yourself, usually with the Node dot js server that the standalone Next.js build produces, on your own infrastructure — a container, a virtual machine, or your own Kubernetes setup. This gives you full control over the hosting environment and avoids being tied to a specific vendor, but it means you're responsible for wiring up things Vercel handles automatically, like a caching layer for revalidated pages, a CDN in front of static assets, and image optimization infrastructure.

Some App Router features have specific infrastructure requirements to work exactly the same way outside Vercel — for example, incremental static regeneration relies on being able to write updated pages somewhere and serve them consistently across all your running instances, which needs deliberate setup if you're running multiple server instances behind a load balancer, since a naive setup could have each instance caching things independently and disagreeing with each other.

Neither approach is universally better — Vercel trades some control and potential cost at scale for convenience and tight framework integration, while self-hosting trades initial setup effort and ongoing infrastructure ownership for more control over cost and where the app actually runs.
bonus: Next.js does ship an official standalone output mode specifically designed to make self-hosting easier, producing a minimal, self-contained server folder with just the files needed to run the app, which is meant to make Docker-based self-hosting much more practical than it used to be.

question: Question 44. How do you manage environment configuration across development, staging, and production for a Next.js app?
answer:
I keep environment-specific values, like API base URLs and secret keys, entirely out of the codebase and set them through environment variables, using local dot env files for development and the hosting platform's own environment variable settings for staging and production.

For local development, a dot env local file — which is git-ignored by default in a standard Next.js setup — holds real values for a developer's machine, while a checked-in dot env example file documents which variables exist without containing any real secret values.

For staging and production, the actual values are set directly in the hosting platform's configuration — Vercel's project settings, for example, let you set different values per environment, so the same codebase automatically picks up the right database URL or API key depending on where it's deployed, with no code changes needed between environments.

I also try to keep the number of environment-specific branches in the code itself as small as possible — mostly just the values themselves should differ between environments, not the actual logic, since environment-dependent branching in application code tends to become a source of bugs that only show up in one specific environment.
bonus: It's worth double checking early on which environment variables need the next public prefix versus which should stay server-only, since getting that wrong for a secret is a very easy way to accidentally ship a private key into the client bundle.

question: Question 45. How does incremental static regeneration behave differently in production compared to what you might see in local development?
answer:
In local development, Next.js typically regenerates pages on every single request, since the whole point of dev mode is showing you the latest code and content change immediately without needing to think about caching at all.

In production, incremental static regeneration behaves the way it's actually designed to — a page is served from its cached version until the revalidation window passes or an on-demand revalidation is triggered, and only then does Next.js regenerate it, serving the still-cached version to any requests that happen while the regeneration is in progress.

This means a bug in revalidation logic can be very easy to miss during local development, since dev mode's behavior doesn't really exercise the caching path the same way production traffic does — testing revalidation properly usually means running an actual production build locally, or checking it in a real staging environment, rather than trusting what you see in the normal dev server.

In a self-hosted setup running multiple server instances, this also raises a coordination question — each instance needs to either share the same cache storage, or you need to accept that different instances might serve a page at slightly different levels of freshness until they all catch up, which isn't a problem you'd normally even notice while working with a single local dev server.
bonus: Running npm run build followed by the production start command locally, instead of relying on the dev server, is a good habit specifically for verifying caching and revalidation behavior before trusting it will work correctly once deployed.

question: Question 46. How would you set up monitoring and observability for a Next.js app in production?
answer:
I'd want visibility into a few different layers: actual errors happening in the app, performance metrics as real users experience them, and enough logging on the server side to debug issues after the fact.

For errors, I'd use an error tracking tool that supports both client and server code, since Next.js apps have errors that can happen in either place — a Client Component throwing during an interaction, or a Server Component or Server Action throwing during a request. Wiring up error tracking in both places, rather than just the client side like a traditional single-page app might, is important given how much logic now runs on the server side of the App Router.

For performance, I'd track Core Web Vitals from real users, using the built-in reporting hook Next.js provides, sent to an analytics or monitoring service, since lab testing alone doesn't tell you what actual users on real devices and networks are experiencing.

For server-side logging, especially around things like Server Actions, Route Handlers, and any caching or revalidation logic, I'd add structured logging with enough context — which route, which cache tag, what triggered a revalidation — so that when something looks stale or broken in production, there's a trail to actually diagnose why, instead of only being able to guess based on symptoms.
bonus: Because a lot of App Router logic now runs on the server rather than the client, it's worth explicitly checking that whatever monitoring setup a team already has actually captures server-side errors and logs, and isn't just a leftover client-side-only setup from an older single-page app.

question: Question 47. How do you configure redirects and rewrites in a Next.js app?
answer:
Redirects and rewrites can both be configured centrally in the Next config file, through redirect and rewrite functions that return a list of rules, each specifying a source path to match and a destination to send it to.

A redirect actually changes the URL in the browser — the user's address bar updates to the new path, and it's visible to search engines as an actual redirect, which matters for things like permanently moving a page and wanting search engines to update their index to the new URL, typically using a permanent redirect for that case.

A rewrite serves content from a different path without changing the URL the user sees at all — it's useful for things like quietly proxying a path to a different internal route, or mapping a clean public URL to a more complex internal route structure, while keeping the URL bar showing the original clean path the whole time.

For conditional cases that need to check something at request time, like a cookie or a header, rather than a fixed static rule, middleware is the better tool, since the config-based redirects and rewrites are evaluated based on the path itself and some limited conditions, while middleware gives you full access to the incoming request to make a more dynamic decision.
bonus: It's easy to forget that redirects configured in the Next config file are evaluated at build time in some cases and don't always support every kind of dynamic condition — for genuinely dynamic redirect logic based on live data, doing the redirect inside middleware or directly inside a page or Server Action is usually more reliable.

question: Question 48. How does internationalized routing work in Next.js?
answer:
In the older Pages Router, Next.js had built-in internationalized routing support directly in the config — you'd list your supported locales, and Next.js would automatically handle locale-prefixed paths, detect a user's preferred locale, and make the current locale available through the router.

In the App Router, that built-in mechanism was removed, and internationalized routing is instead something you build yourself, usually with middleware and route structure, combined with a translation library like next intl or a similar package designed for the App Router.

A common pattern is to add a locale-based dynamic segment at the top of your route structure, like a bracket locale bracket folder wrapping all your other routes, so every path naturally includes a locale prefix, such as slash en slash about or slash fr slash about. Middleware then handles detecting the user's preferred locale from something like the accept-language header or a cookie, and redirecting them to the right locale-prefixed path if they land on a URL without one.

From there, translation content itself is usually loaded per locale inside layouts or pages, often using a dedicated internationalization library that integrates with Server Components, rather than Next.js providing this out of the box the way it used to.
bonus: Because this moved from a built-in framework feature to something the community fills in with libraries, it's worth checking which specific internationalization library a project uses, since the exact patterns for loading translations and switching locale differ meaningfully between them.

question: Question 49. What are some common authentication patterns in the App Router?
answer:
A common pattern is checking authentication in middleware, reading a session cookie or token, and redirecting unauthenticated users to a login page before they ever reach a protected route's actual page component — this centralizes the check in one place rather than repeating it inside every protected page.

Another layer is checking the session again inside the actual Server Component or layout for a protected section, since middleware alone can sometimes be bypassed or might not have access to everything needed for a more detailed authorization check, like checking a specific permission rather than just whether the user is logged in at all. This defense-in-depth approach — checking in middleware for the common case, and checking again where it actually matters for authorization — is generally safer than relying on just one layer.

For the actual sign-in flow, a Server Action is a natural fit for handling a login form submission — verifying credentials, creating a session, and setting a cookie, all on the server, without needing a separate client-side fetch call to a login API route.

Many teams also use a dedicated authentication library built with the App Router in mind, which handles a lot of these details — secure cookie handling, session refresh, provider integrations for things like social login — rather than building all of that from scratch, since getting the security details right by hand is easy to get subtly wrong.
bonus: A subtle but important detail is that reading cookies or headers inside a Server Component makes that component dynamically rendered rather than static, since its output can now depend on request-specific data — that's worth remembering when a page you expected to be statically generated turns out not to be, because of an auth check inside it.

question: Question 50. What are some common pitfalls teams run into when scaling a Next.js app, and how would you approach fixing them?
answer:
One common pitfall is overusing client components out of habit from older React experience, which quietly bloats the JavaScript bundle and hydration cost across the whole app. The fix is usually a deliberate audit — going through key pages and asking, for each client component, whether it actually needs to be one, and pushing that boundary down or removing it where it doesn't.

Another is misunderstanding the caching layers — teams sometimes get surprised by content that seems stuck being stale, or by a page unexpectedly hitting the backend on every request, because they didn't fully account for how fetch caching, the full route cache, and the router cache interact. Working through this usually means deliberately testing a production build, not just relying on the dev server, and reading closely which specific Next.js version's caching defaults apply.

Another is not planning for how incremental static regeneration and revalidation behave in a multi-instance self-hosted deployment, where different server instances can end up disagreeing about what's cached — this usually needs a shared caching layer or an explicit decision to accept some inconsistency, rather than assuming it behaves the same as it does with a single instance or on a platform like Vercel that handles it for you.

At a broader level, a lot of scaling problems come from treating every page the same way instead of intentionally choosing a rendering strategy per route based on its actual requirements — going back and revisiting that decision for the highest-traffic or slowest pages, rather than leaving every page on whatever default it happened to start with, is usually where the biggest wins are.
bonus: When a team hits one of these problems for the first time, it's usually a good moment to actually document the rendering and caching strategy per major route in the app, since without that written down, decisions like these tend to just live in one or two people's heads and get relearned the hard way by everyone else.
